name: Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop from Daroca
name_original: Agualleve de Anento y Bágena por la Vía Verde Santander-Mediterráneo — ida y vuelta desde Daroca (CidCT)
description: Cycle the Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop from Daroca, a moderate 17.7-mile route featuring unique springs and historic villages.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Daroca offers diverse landscapes within the Iberian System, characterized by varied topography and altitudes often exceeding 1000 meters. The region features the picturesque Jiloca River Valley, extensive medieval walled enclosures, and unique natural features like the Aguallueve de Anento. Cyclists can explore routes that traverse quiet roads, some unpaved segments, and charming villages, providing a blend of natural beauty and historical interest. Best touring cycling routes around Daroca The most popular touring cycling route is Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop from Daroca, a 17.7 miles (28.5 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 2 minutes to complete. This route features the unique Aguallueve de Anento spring and follows a Vía Verde segment. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is The Three Taifas Trail, an easy 16.7 miles (26.8 km) path. This route offers an easy touring experience along the Jiloca River, passing through charming villages. Local touring cyclists also love the Carinena – Puerto de Huerva loop from Langa del Castillo, a 39.9 miles (64.3 km) trail leading through the varied geography of the Iberian Cordillera, often completed in about 4 hours 11 minutes. Touring cycling around Daroca is defined by its medieval walled enclosure, the Jiloca River Valley, and the mountainous terrain of the Iberian System. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Daroca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 4 reviews. More than 70 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Daroca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Daroca?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes available around Daroca. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy rides along river valleys to more challenging excursions through mountainous terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Daroca?
A: The terrain around Daroca is diverse, featuring the varied topography of the Iberian System. You can expect routes that traverse quiet paved roads, some unpaved segments, and charming villages. Many routes include altitudes above 1000 meters, offering a mix of climbs and descents, often under favorable weather conditions.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Daroca offers easy touring routes perfect for beginners or families. For example, &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e956698744/the-three-taifas-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Three Taifas Trail&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt; is an easy 16.7-mile (26.8 km) path that provides a gentle touring experience along the picturesque Jiloca River, passing through charming villages.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I can see while cycling in Daroca?
A: Cyclists can explore a rich blend of natural beauty and historical landmarks. A unique natural monument is &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5088573" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguallueve de Anento&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt;, a continuously dripping spring creating impressive limestone formations. Historically, Daroca is known for its extensive medieval walled enclosure, including impressive stone gateways like &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5765158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerta Baja de Daroca&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt;, and the ruins of a medieval castle offering panoramic views. The Jiloca River Valley itself is a scenic highlight.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Daroca area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. An example is the &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993369169/aguallueve-de-anento-and-via-verde-loop-from-daroca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop from Daroca&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt;, a moderate 17.7-mile (28.5 km) trail that allows you to start and finish in the same location while exploring the unique Aguallueve de Anento spring and a Vía Verde segment.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Daroca?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, the blend of natural beauty with historical sites, and the variety of routes suitable for different ability levels. The opportunity to cycle through charming villages and along the Jiloca River is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Daroca offers challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists. For instance, the &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20144078" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carinena – Puerto de Huerva loop from Langa del Castillo&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt; is a difficult 39.9-mile (64.3 km) trail with significant elevation gain (870m), leading through the varied geography of the Iberian Cordillera.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Daroca?
A: Daroca generally offers a favorable climate for cycling with minimal wind or rain, making it enjoyable for much of the year. The region's location in the Iberian System means routes often traverse altitudes above 1000 meters, suggesting spring and autumn are ideal for comfortable temperatures, though summer can also be pleasant, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Can I find accommodation and places to eat near the cycling routes in Daroca?
A: Daroca and its surrounding charming villages offer opportunities for cultural immersion, which includes local accommodation and eateries. While specific cafes or pubs directly on every trail are not guaranteed, the towns and villages along the routes provide options for refreshments and overnight stays, allowing cyclists to experience traditional Aragonese life.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through the historic town of Daroca itself?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate or start from the historic town of Daroca, allowing you to explore its medieval heritage. The &lt;strong&gt;&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143702" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerta Baja de Daroca – Daroca loop from Estación de Daroca&lt;/a&gt;&lt;/strong&gt; is an example of a moderate route that begins near Daroca and offers a chance to see its historic features.

Q: Is it possible to combine cycling with exploring historical sites in Daroca?
A: Absolutely. Daroca is renowned for its rich history. Many cycling routes pass through or near the town's medieval walled enclosure, its castle ruins, and significant religious structures like the Church of Santo Domingo de Silos. This allows cyclists to easily combine physical activity with cultural exploration and visits to historical landmarks.
tips: The fact of being a border city between regions and kingdoms has marked the history of one of the most suffered but at the same time beautiful cities in Aragon. At this point we find ourselves at the Lower Gate that welcomes us to the city through which we can tour its walls, built at different times, up to the old castle from which we can enjoy an impressive panoramic view of Daroca.

In this charming town, the Mudejar tower of its parish church stands out above all. You can also find numerous palatial houses (one of them, Casa Lucías, with the longest façade in Teruel, in danger of demolition), the Gothic-Baroque convent of San Valentín and, crowning the town, its 14th-century Gothic-civil castle and Templar origins.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop from Daroca?
faqs.answer: This cycling loop is rated as moderate. While some sections are paved (state road, road, cycleway, street), a significant portion consists of unpaved paths, which can be challenging for cyclists. Good physical fitness is recommended, especially given the total distance and elevation gain.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and natural features along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery. A key highlight is the unique Aguallueve de Anento, a natural spring where continuously dripping water creates spectacular moss and stone formations. You'll also pass through Anento Village, known for its picturesque charm and historic castle, and cycle along sections of the Santander–Mediterranean Nature Trail, which offers car-free cycling on repurposed railway lines.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The loop is approximately 28.5 km (17.7 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 2 minutes to complete by touring bicycle, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed during various seasons, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with milder temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Summer can be hot, so starting early in the day is advisable.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The loop starts and ends in Daroca. You should be able to find parking within the town to begin your ride. Specific parking areas for the Vía Verde sections may also be available.

faqs.question: Is this cycling loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed on Vías Verdes and many trails, it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially in villages like Anento and around the Aguallueve, and to ensure they are comfortable with the moderate cycling distance and varied terrain.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Aguallueve de Anento and Vía Verde Loop. Access to the Vía Verde sections and natural areas is generally free.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can find options for food and drink in Daroca at the start/end of your ride. The charming village of Anento may also offer some local establishments. Some repurposed railway stations along Vías Verdes have been converted into cafes or hotels, so keep an eye out for those.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of the Vía Verde Santander-Mediterráneo (Campo de Daroca - Jiloca). It also intersects with parts of Las Tres Taifas, Río Jiloca y Camino de la Azucarera, and the Camino del Cid (Senderista) and GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and varied terrain, including unpaved sections, this route is best suited for families with older children who are experienced cyclists and have good physical fitness. The Vía Verde sections are generally gentler, but the overall loop requires stamina.

faqs.question: Can I rent a bike near the starting point in Daroca?
faqs.answer: While specific bike rental shops in Daroca are not detailed, it's advisable to check local tourism information or search online for bike rental services in the area before your visit, especially if you don't bring your own.
